content.text: Definition of Done for GenAI Feature Delivery

A Definition of Done (DoD) is the shared agreement on what 'complete' means before a feature ships to production. Without a DoD, teams ship features that pass functional tests but lack operational readiness: missing observability, unreviewed prompts, no rollback procedure, and security gaps discovered in production.

GenAI features require a DoD that goes beyond standard software delivery. This document provides a seven-domain checklist covering functional correctness, prompt governance, security, observability, testing, cost/performance, and documentation/compliance. Every item must be checked before a GenAI feature is marked done. Items that genuinely do not apply must be marked N/A with a brief reason — blanks are never acceptable.

attrs.alt: Definition of Done checklist — seven domains for GenAI feature delivery

content.text: Domain 1: Functional Correctness

Functional correctness for a GenAI feature means more than 'the happy path works'. It means the model produces acceptable outputs across the range of expected inputs, handles edge cases gracefully, and fails in predictable ways when the model cannot complete the task.
Check
Definition of pass
Common failure mode
Golden eval set passes (≥85%)
CI eval gate shows ≥85% keyword match rate on all eval cases in the YAML
Eval set too small to catch regressions; template changed without updating evals
Edge cases covered in tests
At least 2 non-happy-path eval cases: empty input, very long input, ambiguous input
Only happy path tested; edge cases surface in production
stop_reason handled
Code checks stop_reason == 'end_turn'; handles 'max_tokens' separately
Truncated output silently treated as complete; downstream parsing fails
Output validated before use
JSON parsed with try/except; schema validated with Pydantic or equivalent
Model returns prose when JSON expected; uncaught exception in production
Fallback path tested
Unit test injects provider error; asserts fallback response or graceful error message
Provider outage causes unhandled exception visible to users
Latency p95 within budget
Load test or staging profiling shows p95 latency ≤ product requirement (e.g. 3s for UI, 30s for batch)
Latency acceptable in testing but exceeds target under production load
Domain 2: Prompt Governance

A GenAI feature is not done until its prompt templates are properly governed. An inline f-string that works in development is not acceptable in production — it has no version history, no review record, and cannot be rolled back independently of the application code.
All prompt templates must be in YAML files under src/prompts/, not inline strings in application code.
The safety baseline clause (version 2.1 or later) must appear verbatim in every system prompt of every customer-facing template.

The prompt governance PR must be merged before the feature PR can merge. The two PRs are reviewed separately: the prompt PR by the prompt governance process; the feature PR by the standard code review process.
The CHANGELOG.md for each modified template must describe what changed, why, and what callers need to do differently (for MAJOR bumps).
The version pointer in src/config/prompt_versions.py must be updated to point to the version that will run in production.
Domain 3: Security

Security for GenAI features has distinct failure modes that do not appear in traditional web applications. The checklist items here address the GenAI-specific attack surface: prompt injection, model output injection, and unbounded cost attacks.

# Pre-ship security verification: run against staging before production deploy
import subprocess, sys

checks = [
    # No API keys in source
    ["detect-secrets", "scan", "src/", "--baseline", ".secrets.baseline"],
    # No hardcoded model names
    ["python", "scripts/check_hardcoded_model.py"],
    # Prompt lint (safety baseline, no secrets, no model names)
    ["python", "scripts/lint_system_prompts.py", "src/prompts/"],
    # Injection probe eval
    ["python", "scripts/run_prompt_evals.py", "--injection-only",
     "--model", "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", "--fail-threshold", "1.0"],
]

for cmd in checks:
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True)
    status = "PASS" if result.returncode == 0 else "FAIL"
    print(f"[{status}] {' '.join(cmd)}")
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(result.stdout.decode())
        sys.exit(1)
Domain 4: Observability
Observability is a done criterion, not a post-launch improvement. A GenAI feature without instrumentation is opaque: when quality degrades or costs spike, there is no data to diagnose the problem.
Check
How to verify
Tool / reference
OTel span per LLM call
Trace a test request; span visible in Jaeger/Tempo
OpenTelemetry SDK
llm.provider/model in attrs
Span attributes include llm.provider and llm.model
src/llm/tracing.py: traced_chat()
Token counts captured
Span attrs: llm.tokens.input, llm.tokens.output
resp.usage.input_tokens / output_tokens
Cost metric emitted
Metrics explorer: llm_cost_usd_total counter present
src/llm/metrics.py: COST_COUNTER
Latency histogram metric
Metrics explorer: llm_request_duration_seconds present
LATENCY_HISTOGRAM
Alert configured
Alert manager shows rule: llm_error_rate > 0.05
alerts/llm.yaml in ops repo
Dashboard panel added
Grafana dashboard has panel for this feature's LLM traffic
ops/dashboards/genai.json
Domains 5–7: Testing, Cost, Documentation
Domain 5: Testing
Unit tests mock at the SDK adapter boundary (AnthropicAdapter or OpenAIAdapter class). Tests that call the live LLM are integration tests, not unit tests, and must not run in the unit test suite.

Retry logic must be tested by injecting synthetic 429 and 5xx responses. Assert that: the retry runs the expected number of times; the backoff delay is applied; the final result is either a success (if the retry succeeds) or a specific error (if all retries fail).
The max_tokens truncation path must be tested. Inject a mock response with stop_reason='max_tokens' and assert the correct error is raised or the correct fallback is returned.
If the feature uses tool calls, test that the tool allowlist is enforced: inject a mock model response that requests a tool not in the allowlist and assert it is rejected.
Domain 6: Cost and Performance
Document the expected token budget per request in the PR description: input tokens (system prompt + user message + context) and expected max_tokens for the response.
The engineering manager must approve the cost target before the feature ships. For features with > $100/day estimated LLM cost, approval is required from the VP of Engineering.

If the feature calls the LLM in a batch loop, a concurrency cap (asyncio.Semaphore or bounded thread pool) is mandatory. Uncapped concurrency can saturate the provider's rate limit and generate a single-hour bill that exceeds a month's budget.
Latency p95 must be measured under load representative of production traffic. A feature that takes 2s at 1 req/s may take 15s at 50 req/s if the provider rate-limits.
Domain 7: Documentation and Compliance
An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is required if the feature introduces a new model, a new provider, or a new data flow involving personal data.

The on-call runbook must be updated with: how to identify whether an incident is LLM-related (log pattern, metric threshold); how to roll back the prompt to the previous version; how to disable the LLM call and fall back to the non-LLM path if available.

If the feature processes personal data (names, emails, medical records, financial data), the compliance scope must be documented: which regulation applies (GDPR, HIPAA, EU AI Act), whether a DPIA/PIA is required, and who approved the processing.
The data flow diagram for the system must be updated to show LLM API calls, what data is sent, whether it is logged by the provider, and the data retention policy.
Adapting the DoD for Feature Size
Not every GenAI change is a full feature. A one-line prompt wording change carries a different scope than a new LLM-powered module. The DoD is adapted based on the change classification.
Change classification
Required DoD domains
Skippable with N/A
Prompt PATCH (wording tweak, eval addition)
Domains 1–2 only
Domains 3–7 may be N/A if no code changed
Prompt MINOR (optional variable, wording change)
Domains 1–3 (security review if flagged)
Domains 4–7 N/A if no code or infrastructure changed
Prompt MAJOR (breaking variable change)
Domains 1–3 + Domain 7 (ADR if callers change)
Domains 4–6 N/A if no code changed
New LLM integration (code + prompt)
All 7 domains required
N/A only for individual checks that cannot apply (e.g. no batch job → no concurrency cap check)
Model swap (same prompt, different model)
Domains 1, 3, 4, 6 (re-run eval, check cost, update metrics)
Domain 2 N/A if prompt unchanged; Domains 5, 7 N/A if no code or arch change

When in doubt about which classification applies, default to the full DoD. The cost of completing a few extra checklist items is lower than the cost of a production incident caused by a skipped check.
Gating the DoD in Sprint and Release Ceremonies

The DoD checklist is most effective when it is integrated into the team's existing sprint and release ceremonies, rather than treated as a separate audit step at the end. The following cadence embeds DoD review into the points where teams already review work.
Sprint review gate

At sprint review, each GenAI story is demoed as normal. Immediately after the demo, the author reads out the DoD checklist status: which items are checked, which are N/A (with reason), and whether any items are incomplete. An incomplete DoD item means the story is not accepted — it returns to the backlog as a carry-over with the incomplete items as explicit tasks.

The product owner accepts a story, not the tech lead. But the tech lead has veto power: they can block acceptance if any non-N/A DoD item is unchecked, even if the feature is functionally complete. This separation prevents functional completeness from overriding operational readiness.
Pre-release checklist
Before any GenAI feature is deployed to production, the tech lead runs a final pre-release check. This is distinct from the per-story DoD: it covers the deployment itself, not the individual feature.

## Pre-Release Checklist: GenAI Feature Deploy

### Deployment readiness
- [ ] All feature PRs merged to main (code + prompt governance PRs)
- [ ] CI green on main (unit tests, integration tests, eval suite)
- [ ] Staging deploy verified: requests flowing, metrics emitting, no errors
- [ ] Feature flag configured (if using staged rollout)
- [ ] Rollback plan documented and tested in staging

### Observability readiness
- [ ] Grafana dashboard shows traffic from staging deploy
- [ ] Alert rules active in alert manager
- [ ] On-call engineer briefed on new LLM-related alerts

### Cost readiness
- [ ] Cost-per-request estimate reviewed by EM
- [ ] Concurrency cap set if feature is high-volume
- [ ] Cost alert configured (fires if daily spend exceeds approved limit)

### Security readiness
- [ ] Security review merged (if applicable)
- [ ] Rate limiter active on public endpoint
- [ ] Injection probe eval passed

### Communication
- [ ] Runbook updated with new LLM feature section
- [ ] Changelog entry in CHANGELOG.md for the release
- [ ] Stakeholders notified of new LLM capability
Tracking DoD Compliance Over Time
A DoD that is enforced inconsistently degrades into a nominal process that no one trusts. The following metrics let engineering managers track whether the DoD is actually being applied.
Metric
Healthy range
Alert threshold
Action if alerting
Stories shipped without full DoD sign-off (per sprint)
Tech lead investigates each instance; carry-overs go to DoD item backlog
DoD items marked N/A (% of total items across all stories)
Review if N/A use is legitimate or if teams are using N/A to skip checks
Observability items missed (found post-launch)
Add automated observability gate to CI (check for OTel instrumentation)
Security items missed (found in security review or pentest)
Review how the item was N/A'd or missed; add automated check if possible
Prompt governance PR merged after feature PR
This is a process violation — enforce ordering in branch protection rules

# scripts/dod_compliance_report.py — weekly DoD compliance summary
# Reads from a DoD tracking spreadsheet or Jira custom fields
import json
from pathlib import Path

def load_dod_records(file: str) -> list[dict]:
    # Each record: story_id, sprint, items_checked, items_na, items_missing
    return json.loads(Path(file).read_text())

def report(records: list[dict]) -> None:
    total_stories = len(records)
    fully_done = sum(1 for r in records if r["items_missing"] == 0)
    has_na = sum(1 for r in records if r["items_na"] > 0)
    na_items = sum(r["items_na"] for r in records)
    total_items = sum(r["items_checked"] + r["items_na"] + r["items_missing"] for r in records)

    print(f"Stories this sprint:         {total_stories}")
    print(f"Fully done (0 missing):      {fully_done} ({100*fully_done//max(total_stories,1)}%)")
    print(f"Stories with N/A items:      {has_na}")
    print(f"N/A item rate:               {100*na_items//max(total_items,1)}%")
    print(f"Stories with missing items:  {total_stories - fully_done} (CARRY-OVERS)")

records = load_dod_records("dod-records-sprint-42.json")
report(records)
The DoD as a Learning Tool

Beyond compliance, the DoD is a learning artefact. Engineers who are new to GenAI development use the checklist as a guide to what good looks like — each item is a lesson in disguise about what failure mode it prevents.
Onboarding new engineers

When onboarding a new engineer to a GenAI team, walk through the DoD with them on their first feature. For each item, explain: what it checks, why it matters (what failure mode it prevents), and how to verify it. This is more effective than a lecture on GenAI best practices — it is contextual, applied, and immediately actionable.
Post-incident DoD review

After every production incident involving a GenAI feature, the post-mortem should include a DoD audit: which DoD item, if checked properly, would have caught the root cause before production? If no existing item would have caught it, add a new item to the DoD.
Model output used in SQL without validation → add 'Output not used in SQL/shell' to Domain 3.
No alert when error rate spiked → add 'Alert configured (error rate)' to Domain 4.
Prompt injection via user content → add 'Injection probe eval present' and 'User input delimited' to Domain 3.
Provider outage caused unhandled exception → add 'Fallback path tested' to Domain 1.
Token cost 10× expected due to no concurrency cap → add 'Concurrency cap on batch jobs' to Domain 6.
A DoD that grows from real incidents is more resilient than one designed in advance. Every item in the current DoD has a production incident behind it.
Tooling: Enforcing the DoD in GitHub
Several DoD checks can be enforced automatically in GitHub, reducing reliance on manual checklist completion and ensuring that compliance is structural rather than behavioural.

# .github/pull_request_template.md — DoD checklist embedded in every GenAI PR
# GitHub renders this as a Markdown checklist in the PR description

## GenAI Feature PR — Definition of Done

> Check all items or mark N/A with a reason. Blanks block merge.

### 1. Functional Correctness
- [ ] Golden eval set passes (≥85%) — attach CI eval report link
- [ ] stop_reason checked in code
- [ ] Output validated before use (JSON parsed safely)
- [ ] Fallback path unit-tested

### 2. Prompt Governance
- [ ] Prompt template in YAML (not inline f-string)
- [ ] Safety baseline v2.1+ present
- [ ] Prompt governance PR merged: #___

### 3. Security
- [ ] No API key in source or prompt (detect-secrets passed)
- [ ] User input delimited if applicable
- [ ] Rate limiter on public endpoint
- [ ] PII masked in logs

### 4. Observability
- [ ] OTel span instrumented
- [ ] Cost metric emitted
- [ ] Alert configured — link: ___

### 5. Testing
- [ ] Unit tests mock at adapter boundary
- [ ] Retry (429, 5xx) and truncation (max_tokens) tested

### 6. Cost and Performance
- [ ] Token budget documented: input=___ output=___
- [ ] max_tokens sized to task
- [ ] Cost target approved by EM: ___

### 7. Documentation and Compliance
- [ ] Runbook updated
- [ ] Compliance scope documented (or N/A: ___)
- [ ] ADR filed if applicable (or N/A: ___)

**Tech lead sign-off before production:** @___

The PR template makes the DoD checklist the default description for every PR in the GenAI codebase. GitHub renders the checkboxes as interactive controls, so reviewers can check items without editing Markdown. Branch protection can require that a tech lead has commented 'DoD: approved' before the PR merges, enforcing the final gate.
Common DoD Anti-Patterns

Teams that are new to formalised DoD processes often fall into predictable patterns that undermine the checklist without obviously breaking it. These are the most common anti-patterns observed in GenAI teams.
Anti-pattern
How it manifests
Consequence
Fix
Checkbox farming
Author checks all items quickly without verifying them; PR description shows all green before CI finishes
False confidence; items that should fail are passed
Tech lead spot-checks 2–3 items per PR; ask 'how did you verify this?' for each spot-checked item
N/A as default
Team marks most security and compliance items N/A without documented reasons
Security and compliance gaps accumulate silently
Require a written reason for every N/A; tech lead reviews N/A reasons during sprint review
DoD at end of sprint
Stories are coded in week 1–2, DoD is filled out in week 3 as an afterthought
Items that require rework discovered too late to complete in the sprint
Treat DoD as a parallel checklist, not a post-coding step; check observability and testing items before coding is considered complete
Prompt governance after code merge
Feature code is merged but the prompt governance PR is still open or in review
Production runs unreviewed prompts; rollback requires two PRs
Branch protection: feature PR cannot merge until the prompt governance PR link is filled in and verified as merged
DoD not updated after incidents
Post-mortem identifies a gap, recommends a new DoD item, but the DoD template is never updated
Same gap recurs in the next feature
Post-mortem action item owner: update DoD template within 5 business days of incident close

The most dangerous anti-pattern is 'checkbox farming' because it creates the appearance of compliance without the substance. Spot-checks by the tech lead are the most effective defence: they are low-cost (2 minutes per item) and create accountability without adding process overhead.
Summary
The GenAI DoD has seven domains: Functional Correctness, Prompt Governance, Security, Observability, Testing, Cost/Performance, and Documentation/Compliance.
Every item must be explicitly checked or marked N/A with a reason. Blanks are not acceptable — a blank item signals the check was overlooked, not that it passed.
Prompt governance is a blocking prerequisite: the prompt template PR must be merged (with domain reviewer approval and security review if flagged) before the feature PR can merge.
Observability is a ship requirement: OTel spans, cost metrics, and error-rate alerts must be wired before production deploy, not added as post-launch improvements.
The DoD is adapted by change classification: prompt-only changes (PATCH/MINOR) require fewer domains than a full new LLM integration.
A feature is not done until the tech lead has signed off on the full DoD checklist and all gate PRs have merged.
